g.data-narration: Welcome to Network Layer Services - the backbone of internet communication
The Network Layer sits at Layer 3, enabling communication across different networks globally
Four essential functions: logical addressing, routing, forwarding, and fragmentation enable packet delivery
Logical addressing uses hierarchical IP addresses, different from physical MAC addresses, enabling network routing
The IPv4 header contains 14 fields including version, TTL, protocol, and source/destination addresses
Classful IPv4 addressing divides the address space into five classes: A, B, C for unicast, D for multicast, E reserved
Subnetting divides networks using masks; CIDR uses prefix notation like /24 for efficient address allocation
g.text: Divides large network into smaller subnets
Replaces classful A, B, C boundaries

g.data-narration: For 192.168.100.0/24 divided into 4 subnets, we borrow 2 bits creating /26 subnets with 64 addresses each
NAT translates private IPs to public IPs, allowing multiple devices to share one public address
Three main routing protocol types: distance-vector (RIP), link-state (OSPF), and path-vector (BGP) serve different network scales
ICMP supports IP by reporting errors and providing diagnostics; ping uses echo request/reply messages
g.text: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) - Network Layer Support

g.data-narration: Routers use routing tables with destination networks, next hops, and interfaces to forward packets toward destinations
Longest Prefix Match selects routes by comparing the most network bits; longer matches take precedence
